nativeRange.habitat: Inhabits caves, ledges, and crevices on tropical coral and rocky reefs, generally between 3 and 46 m depth but typically encountered between 15 and 46 m. It is a secretive species that stays close to the bottom, spending most of its time sheltering inside reef structures rather than swimming in the open.

diet.notes: Has been documented accepting a range of meaty preparations once acclimated, including vitamin-enriched mysis shrimp, finely chopped seafood, krill, and quality frozen carnivore mixes. Generally advised to feed small amounts multiple times daily to suit a relatively high-metabolism planktivore-leaning predator. Tends to ambush prey from cover rather than chase food in the open, so target-feeding near a favored cave can help during acclimation.

avoidWith: other Liopropoma basslets (unless a confirmed mated pair in a larger system)
tiny gobies and other bite-sized fish
large aggressive predators (lionfish, large groupers, moray eels)
boisterous tankmates that out-compete for food

summary: A small, cave-dwelling Indo-Pacific reef basslet (Liopropoma mitratum) with a pastel pink body and a bold yellow caudal margin. Generally considered reef-safe and hardy once acclimated, but tends to spend most of its time tucked into rockwork.
careNotes:
The Yellowmargin Basslet — also widely traded as the Yellowtail Reef Basslet, Liopropoma Reef Basslet, or Mitratus Reef Basslet — is a small Indo-Pacific cave-dweller in the family Liopropomatidae. In the wild it is typically encountered on mesophotic reef slopes between roughly 15 and 46 m, where it shelters under ledges and inside crevices. That deepwater origin shows in the aquarium: the fish is reclusive, prefers shaded rockwork, and tends to be most active during lower-light periods and around feeding times. A mature aquascape with deep caves, overhangs, and shaded recesses is generally advised so the fish has somewhere to feel secure enough to come out and feed in the open.

The Yellowmargin Basslet is generally considered reef-safe, with the usual caveat for the genus: individuals have been reported to pick off very small ornamental shrimp and bite-sized fish, so pairing with sexy shrimp, very small gobies, or post-larval livestock is not recommended. Corals and larger invertebrates are typically left alone. It tends to be peaceful toward unrelated tankmates but is intolerant of conspecifics and of other Liopropoma species — best kept singly unless a confirmed mated pair can be sourced for a noticeably larger system.

The Yellowmargin Basslet has been reported to be a determined jumper, as is common for the genus, and a tightly fitted lid is generally advised. Wild-collected specimens come from depth and can be sensitive to pressure changes at the point of collection, but reputable suppliers tend to handle this well and the species is broadly regarded as hardy once it has fully acclimated to captivity. Several small meals per day is the commonly advised feeding pattern during acclimation.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry baseline (water parameters 72-78°F / pH 8.1-8.4 / SG 1.020-1.025, 30 gal minimum, semi-aggressive temperament, reef-safe with small-crustacean caveat, carnivore diet, 4 in max size). Listed family (Grammidae) was rejected in favor of FishBase's Liopropomatidae. Original /products/yellowmargin-basslet slug 404'd; the canonical /product/3222 page was substituted.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Liopropomatidae, Perciformes/Serranoidei), max body length (9.0 cm TL), depth range (3-46 m, usually 15-46 m), Indo-Pacific distribution (Red Sea to American Samoa and Tuamotu, south to Australia), ledge-dwelling cryptic biology, and IUCN Least Concern status.

additional.name: WorldwideCorals
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (Indo-West Pacific, cave-dwelling on rocky reefs to ~45 m), max size (~4 in), 30 gal minimum when housed singly, hardiness once acclimated, peaceful temperament with small-shrimp caveat, and high-metabolism feeding (small meaty foods twice daily).

additional.name: Reefs4Less
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.022-1.025), semi-aggressive temperament, carnivore diet, and cave/overhang requirement. Reported a much lower 10 gal minimum, which was rejected in favor of the LiveAquaria/WWC 30 gal figure per playbook tank-size convention (bias toward the larger value for territorial cave-dwellers).

additional.name: Reef Builders
additional.notes: Used for genus-wide behavioral context (deep-water preference, extreme reclusiveness, dependence on rockwork and overhangs, preference for lower lighting to increase visibility, morphological similarity to L. pallidum).
